... published in 1499 by Aldus Manutius in 
Venice, is not only one of the most famous illustrated printed books of the 
Italian Renaissance but also, and more importantly, it is considered one 
of the basic precursors of the emblematic culture that lourished in the 
sixteenth century. Its 172 woodcuts are not mere additions to enhance the 
visual aesthetics of the book but rather play a crucial role in understanding 
the story. his proto-emblematic romance was especially successful in 
France, where its adaptations and editions present interesting deviations

In addition, there are woodcuts that function more as allusions to 
things described in the text rather than as actual representations of what 
they refer to. For example, the small empty ship sailing in what might be 
the sea alludes to the couple’s journey to the garden island of Cytherea in 
the company of Cupid, his...

Comparison of the 1499 and 1545 editions of the
Hypnerotomachia Poliphili
How closely does the 1545 edition imitate the 1499? It has
been assumed that they are essentially the same, apart from
the lack of woodcut initial letters and a handful of woodcut
illustrations, and I am unaware of any detailed comparisons
of the two editions. Here are some preliminary observations I
have made.
While the 1545 edition follows the format of the 1499
(gatherings of four folio sheets), in the 1545 edition the...

23 - The illustrator of the French translation of 1546 not only 
endeavoured to give the original Italian woodcuts a greater elegance, 
but also occasionally improved upon fidelity in the illustration of the 
text. Thus in the Italian woodcut of the pyramidal building the 
gorgon's head or 'capo della spaventevola Medusa' referred to in the 
text (folio b ii recto) is not shown whereas it is included in the French 
illustration (A v recto). Elsewhere the French translation has...

Aldus’s type.12 In addition to typographic advances, several
of these early publications contain significant illustrations,
combining the visual and the textual, which reach their
apex in the Hypnerotomachia Poliphili. A 1497 Greek book of
hours had included a woodcut of the Annunciation made
from a block previously used in a 1493 Latin book of hours
by another printer, while the Astronomici Veteres, a collection
of Greek and Latin astronomical texts from 1499, re-used
blocks from Venetian astronomical editions of the 1480s for
its illustrations of the...

symmetry of perspective.” *’ 
Given the Albertian character of the visual thinking propounded in the Hypneroto- 
machia, one would expect the woodcuts to reveal perfect mastery of the perspective tech- 
nique. But, paradoxically, perspectival visual thinking is more evident in the text than in 
the images. How can one explain so many erroneous executions of the foreshortenings and 
alignments associated with perspective? Even an...

... predominant sense and that visual representation was of the 
highest importance for him. 
Among the aspects of the Hypnerotomachia that therefore would be made intellig- 
ible by Alberti’s authorship is its extraordinary visual character. In particular, this would 
explain the quality of the 172 woodcuts it contains. Scholars who have studied the Hypneroto- 
machia have assumed that the woodcuts were conceived in Aldus Manutius’s workshop, at the 
time of its publication in 1499, by someone versed in the works of scholar painters—such as 
Sandro Botticelli, Andrea Mantegna, and Jacopo...
